coredata.dc:title: Waste to wastewater treatment: Graphene quantum dots embedded poly(ethylene terephthalate) membranes for efficient phenol removal

coredata.dc:description: Efficient waste management strategies are essential for addressing environmental degradation and advancing sustainable development. Utilizing waste materials in wastewater treatment presents a promising avenue toward this goal. This study investigates the use of waste carton paper and waste plastic bottles in composite membranes for treating wastewater containing phenol as a contaminant. It introduces a unique approach by utilizing waste carton paper as a bio-source for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and incorporating them as nanofillers in polyethylene terephthalate (PET) membranes derived from recycled plastic bottles. Furthermore, this study addresses the performance and stability of GQDs-PET membranes for smaller dissolvable organics such as phenol in semi-pilot UF systems. An array of characterizations was performed to confirm the successful application of waste carton paper as a bio source for synthesizing GQDs and to study their effect on the PET membrane structure and properties. The pore size, porosity and wetting properties of the GQDs-PET membranes could be flexibly modulated by regulating the contents of the GQDs. The performance stability of fabricated membranes was evaluated using a continuous operating semi-pilot scale cross-flow ultrafiltration (UF) setup. The optimal GQDs-PET membrane showed a higher water flux (24 L/m2.hr.bar) than the pristine PET membrane (9.9 L/m2.hr.bar) and demonstrated enhanced removal performance for phenol, ∼ 94 %, under alkaline feed conditions (pH 9.5) for more than 4 h. The stable phenol removal performance of the GQDs-PET membrane for a longer time can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsions between the membrane surface and phenoxide ion at a higher pH induced by GQDs in the PET membrane structure. These results revealed that the as-prepared GQDs-PET composite membranes possessed great potential for actual wastewater advanced treatment and purification. Additionally, this work supports sustainable development and circular economy principles by repurposing waste plastic bottles and carton paper for efficient wastewater treatment applications.

Supplementary data include a schematic diagram for GQD synthesis, and semi pilot-scale cross-flow ultrafiltration setup. The Supplementary data also includes the particle size histogram based on TEM analysis and a comparative FTIR spectra analysis of the raw waste carton paper.

Characterization of GQDs synthesized from waste carton paper a) Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy analysis, b) X-ray diffraction analysis, c) Raman analysis, d) Thermogravimetric analysis, e) UV-Vis absorption spectrum, f) Fluorescence emission studies, g) Scanning electron microscopy (1 µm, 10 kV), h) Transmission electron microscopy (50 nm, inset (100 nm)).
Surface and cross-sectional scanning electron microscopy images of pristine and GQDs-PET membranes: Pristine (a,e), 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET (b,f), 0.75 wt% GQDs-PET (c,g), 1 wt% GQDs-PET (d,h).

Characterization of pristine and GQDs-PET membranes: (a–d) Atomic force microscopy images for 5 µm × 5 µm membrane area, e) Average surface roughness, f) Membrane porosity and water contact angle measurements (based on three replicates). g) Zeta potential measurements (based on 2 replicates).

a) Water flux and b) % Phenol removal for Pristine and GQDs-PET membranes [experimental conditions: Temperature 25 °C, Pressure 28 psi, feed 20 ppm phenol solution, pH 9.5, Time 0 corresponds to 1st sampling time] c) Phenol removal performance for longer duration (4 h) using optimal 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane [experimental conditions: Temperature 25 °C, Pressure 28 psi, feed 20 ppm phenol solution, pH 9.5] d) effect of pH on phenol removal using optimal 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane [experimental conditions: Temperature 25 °C, Pressure 28 psi, feed 20 ppm phenol solution], e) Schematic of phenol removal mechanism by GQDs-PET membrane.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient waste management strategies are essential for addressing environmental degradation and advancing sustainable development. Utilizing waste materials in wastewater treatment presents a promising avenue toward this goal. This study investigates the use of waste carton paper and waste plastic bottles in composite membranes for treating wastewater containing phenol as a contaminant. It introduces a unique approach by utilizing waste carton paper as a bio-source for the synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and incorporating them as nanofillers in polyethylene terephthalate (PET) membranes derived from recycled plastic bottles. Furthermore, this study addresses the performance and stability of GQDs-PET membranes for smaller dissolvable organics such as phenol in semi-pilot UF systems. An array of characterizations was performed to confirm the successful application of waste carton paper as a bio source for synthesizing GQDs and to study their effect on the PET membrane structure and properties. The pore size, porosity and wetting properties of the GQDs-PET membranes could be flexibly modulated by regulating the contents of the GQDs. The performance stability of fabricated membranes was evaluated using a continuous operating semi-pilot scale cross-flow ultrafiltration (UF) setup. The optimal GQDs-PET membrane showed a higher water flux (24 L/m2.hr.bar) than the pristine PET membrane (9.9 L/m2.hr.bar) and demonstrated enhanced removal performance for phenol, ∼ 94 %, under alkaline feed conditions (pH 9.5) for more than 4 h. The stable phenol removal performance of the GQDs-PET membrane for a longer time can be attributed to the electrostatic repulsions between the membrane surface and phenoxide ion at a higher pH induced by GQDs in the PET membrane structure. These results revealed that the as-prepared GQDs-PET composite membranes possessed great potential for actual wastewater advanced treatment and purification. Additionally, this work supports sustainable development and circular economy principles by repurposing waste plastic bottles and carton paper for efficient wastewater treatment applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Waste management and freshwater availability are critical challenges every country faces. Waste exists as a big problem wherever there is human habitation, and if not properly managed, it endangers public health and the environment. This incurring issue is directly related to societal production and consumption patterns. The United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) and the International Solid Waste Association (ISWA) released a report (February 2024) addressing the urgent need for global actions regarding waste management. According to this report, municipal solid waste (MSW) is projected to increase drastically from 2.3 billion tons in 2023–3.8 billion tons by 2050 (United Nations Environment Programme, 2024). Plastic and paper waste are the two main constituents of MSW, in addition to food, metal, glass, and garden waste. The global average of plastic and paper-based municipal solid waste generation is approximately 12 % and 17 %, respectively (United Nations Environment Programme, 2024).

The quantity and use of plastics have grown significantly since the introduction of the first plastic (Bakelite) in the early 1900s and the emergence of polyethylene in the 1930s (Shen and Worrell, 2014). In 2021, 390.7 million tons of plastics were produced worldwide; 90.2 % (352.3 million tons) were manufactured from fossil fuels, 8.3 % from recycling, and just 1.5 % from bio-based materials (Burelo et al., 2023). As the most widely produced polyester plastic in the world, poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) is well-known for its uses in carpeting, textiles, and packaging (Diao et al., 2023). The annual worldwide production of PET surpasses 70 million tons; however, a mere fraction (< 20 %) of this volume is subjected to recycling processes (Diao et al., 2023). Several practical ways to recover PET include burning it (incineration) and breaking it down physically or chemically. However, most of these plastics are disposed of in landfills and adds to the amount of microplastics in the oceans, resulting in a massive threat to human life and the environment (Pu et al., 2023). Hence, the large amount of plastic produced and its widespread use in various applications substantially contribute to environmental and waste management issues.

Similarly, the enormous amount of paper waste from various nondurable goods such as newspapers, tissue paper, office paper, etc., and packaging such as boxes and cartons significantly damages the environment (Kumar and Verma, 2024). The annual waste paper production from industrial and municipal waste is around 400 million tons. However, only 50–65 % of that waste gets recycled due to poor fiber quality and low tensile strength (Nair et al., 2020). Moreover, the pulp-paper industry produces around 695.7 million m3 of wastewater globally (Haq et al., 2020). The large volumes of sludge produced by pulp-paper industries pose serious disposal challenges. Traditional methods like landfilling, incineration, and composting are widely used but raise environmental concerns due to pollution and greenhouse gas emissions (Kumar et al., 2025; Kumar and Verma, 2023). On the other hand, recycling one ton of waste paper can prevent the need to cut down 17 trees and save 7000 gallons of water (Joshi et al., 2015). Therefore, designing an eco-friendly method of recycling waste paper is essential to avoid environmental degradation.

The ideal strategy to continue benefiting from paper and plastic waste while reducing the environmental damage caused by improper disposal would be implementing a circular economy. Therefore, our research goal is to use waste materials, including PET plastics, waste carton paper, and membranes, to treat wastewater, as freshwater availability is a significant issue. So, this work investigates the application of waste carton paper as a bio source for synthesizing graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the effect of GQDs as nanofillers in PET polymer membrane, derived from waste plastic bottles for removing the small dissolved contaminants from wastewater.

Waste plastic utilization provides an eco-friendly solution to mitigate environmental pollution by transforming discarded plastics into valuable resources for various applications. For instance, Zhao et al. reported a method to convert waste PET plastics into manganese and cobalt (Mn,Co-BDC) metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), effectively repurposing plastic waste. These MOFs are then converted into Mn,Co-doped RuO2 (Ruthenium dioxide), demonstrating excellent performance as an oxygen evolution reaction (OER) catalyst in acidic conditions, significantly improving the hydrogen generation kinetics during water electrolysis (Zhao et al., 2025). Furthermore, the recycled PET in the form of membranes via electrostatic spinning, surface modification, and in-situ deposition has been utilized for the oil-based separation processes such as water-in-oil emulsions, crude oil, gasoline, pump oil, and diesel (Chen et al., 2023; Topuz et al., 2022). However, minimal studies have been reported on PET polymer-based membranes for water treatment because of PET polymers' hydrophobic nature and lower removal performance. Modifying the PET membrane chemistry to a suitable hydrophilic nanofiller is essential to improve the hydrophilicity, water flux, and removal efficiency for wastewater treatment. PET-based membranes modified with cellulose acetate and silica are reported in the literature with a reasonable water flux and removal of humic acid (Mulyati et al., 2018). Moreover, Zeolite NaY fillers in PET polymeric membranes exhibited improved chromium ion rejection (47.8 %) compared to pristine PET membranes (4.07 %) (Ali et al., 2022). However, no work has been reported on the PET-modified membranes for smaller dissolved organic matter. One of the primary reasons for modifying PET membranes with nanofillers is their ability to remove small soluble matter effectively. Therefore, selecting an appropriate nanofiller for membrane modification primarily depends on its chemical selectivity, compatibility with the PET polymer matrix, and hydrophilic properties.

Recently, GQDs received much attention because of their unique properties, such as low cytotoxicity, excellent water dispersibility, high electrical conductivity, good biocompatibility, chemical stability, photoluminescence, and optoelectronic capabilities (Ghaffarkhah et al., 2022; Haque et al., 2018; Iravani and Varma, 2020). Basically, GQDs are collections of 2D graphene nanosheets (1–100 nm in size) with adjustable functional groups. Studies revealed that GQDs are rich in hydrophilic groups and uniform dispersion in the polymer layer that makes them excellent nanofillers for enhancing the performance of membranes for wastewater treatments, especially for dyes removal (Gozali Balkanloo et al., 2023). Bi et al. reported that GQDs as nanofillers improved water permeance with high separation performance for alcian blue and congo red in PES polymer membranes (Bi et al., 2019). Vatanpour et al. noticed that GQDs enhanced antifouling properties in polyvinyl chloride polymer support membranes for dye wastewater treatment (Vatanpour et al., 2020). Gu et al. showed that adding GQDs to anion exchange membranes improves anion separation by enhancing hydrophilic connectivity and reducing attraction for divalent anions (Gu et al., 2025). Also, Bayrami et al. incorporated histidine-functionalized GQDs into the polyamide layer of thin-film composite forward osmosis membranes, significantly enhancing their physicochemical properties, leading to superior antifouling performance and enhanced metal rejection capabilities (Bayrami et al., 2025). Moreover, Li et al. demonstrated that nitrogen-doped N-GQDs play a vital role in enhancing nanofiltration membrane performance by regulating pore size, enabling precise separation through size exclusion and electrostatic interactions (Li et al., 2024). In another study, Li et al. reported that incorporating GQDs into nanofiltration membranes led to the formation of nanoaggregates within the membrane structure, resulting in increased water permeation and dye rejection (Li et al., 2021). Similarly, Fathizadeh et al. found that using N-GQDs as nanofillers in polyamide support membranes enhanced the effective surface area and improved membrane surface hydrophilicity, collectively contributing to the improved water permeation (Fathizadeh et al., 2019). Although GQDs have demonstrated exceptional performance as nanofillers in membranes, their potential to remove small organic pollutants, such as phenol, remains largely unexplored. Also, the preparation of GQDs frequently requires precursor chemicals such as citric acid (Dejpasand et al., 2020), graphite (Joffrion et al., 2019), carbon black (Lu et al., 2017), or potent acids, and their environmentally friendly production using sustainable methods still faces significant challenges (Hasan et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the literature discussed the applicability of biomass, including rice husk (Wang et al., 2016), mango peels (Mahmoud et al., 2022), watermelon rind waste (Rodwihok et al., 2022), Durva grass (Pai et al., 2022), etc., as the precursor for the synthesis of GQDs due to the abundance and affordability of these biomass resources (Kadyan et al., 2023). However, no work has been reported for synthesizing GQDs from waste carton paper as a bio source and its application as nanofillers to PET polymeric membranes derived from waste plastic bottles to remove small soluble contaminants such as phenol.

The main objective of this study is to utilize waste carton paper and plastic bottles for membrane-based wastewater treatment, providing a potential solution for managing these waste materials more effectively. Here, waste carton paper is applied as a bio source for synthesizing GQDs and utilized as nanofillers to improve the properties and performance of PET membranes fabricated from waste plastics. Phenol is a highly toxic and carcinogenic pollutant that poses significant risks to both human and animal health and the environment (Guerra, 2001; Masqué et al., 1998). Due to its small molecular size and high solubility in water, removing phenol from wastewater remains a considerable challenge. Consequently, environmental protection agencies classify phenol and its derivatives as pollutants of major concern. Hence, this study examined the performance of GQDs as nanofillers in the PET polymer matrix for removing phenol from wastewater using a semi-pilot scale cross-flow ultrafiltration (UF) setup. More precisely, this work aims to demonstrate the feasibility and effectiveness of utilizing waste materials, including waste carton paper and plastic bottles, for a sustainable water treatment process for removing soluble small dissolved contaminants.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 97 %), ethanol (99.8 %), sodium metasilicate (NaSiO3, 99 %), phenol (99 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95–97 %), trifluoroacetic acid (TFA, 99 %), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 3 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

GQDs were synthesized from office waste carton paper. First, cellulose was extracted from waste carton paper (Lei et al., 2018), and then, using the extracted cellulose, GQDs were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment (Hatefi et al., 2020). The waste carton paper (45 g) was cut into small pieces (approx. 4 cm2) and deinked using 1.5 % (w/w) NaOH, 3 % (w/w) H2O2, and 5 % (w/w) NaSiO3. The deinked solution was washed with DI water to neutralize the pH and dried in the oven at 105 °C overnight. The dried product was crushed to a fine size using a mechanically driven grinder before acid hydrolysis using 10 % (w/w) H2SO4 to extract cellulose. Subsequently, hydrothermal treatment was performed on the extracted cellulose by dissolving it in 80 ml of water and placed in the Teflon-lined autoclave and oven at 180 °C for 8 h. The solution from the autoclave was collected and dried at 100 °C to obtain the GQDs. The step-wise synthesis of GQDs from waste carton paper is shown in Fig. S1.

The GQDs-embedded PET membranes were fabricated using the phase inversion ( Fig. 1). Waste plastic bottles were used as a source for the recycled PET polymer. The waste plastic bottles were cut into small pieces and dried at 50 °C overnight after washing them with deionized water. Then, 16 wt% of the dried plastic pieces were dissolved in TFA solvent and stirred for 30 minutes. When plastic pieces were completely dissolved in the solvent, the desired concentration of GQDs (0.5, 0.75, 1 wt%) was added to the solution and kept on stirring for 30 minutes. Then, membranes were cast on a clean glass plate with a casting knife of thickness 250 µm. The glass plate with membrane was immediately placed in the co-solvent bath of ethanol/water (20/80 %) mixture. The fabricated membranes were washed with DI water until neutral pH. Finally, the membranes were stored in DI water before use. The pristine PET membrane was fabricated using the above-described procedure without adding GQDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was employed to identify surface functional groups on the synthesized GQDs, utilizing a Bruker ALPHA-Platinum attenuated total reflection (ATR)-FTIR spectrometer to collect transmission spectra ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a benchtop D2 Phaser XRD instrument equipped with an energy-dispersive detector to measure the degree of crystallinity of GQDs with a Cu-Kα (1.54 Å) radiation source. The XRD analysis of GQDs was performed between 10° and 80° ranges with a scanning rate of 5° min−1. The d-spacing for GQDs was calculated by Bragg’s law: λ= 2dsin (θ) where λ is the wavelength of the X-ray beam (0.154 nm), d is the distance between the adjacent layers, θ is the diffraction angle. Raman analysis was also carried out using a Witec Alphas 300 RAS instrument with a 532 nm laser beam wavelength. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed for GQDs on a Perkin Elmers STA6000 instrument in a nitrogen gas environment, with a sample heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1, to assess the material's thermal stability over a temperature range between 20 and 1000 ℃.

The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the GQDs and composite membranes were identified by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using Quanta ESEM 250 equipped with an EDAX Apollo SDD detector and Quanta 3D FEG (SEM/FIB). The GQDs were dispersed in water and were applied on the silicon wafer using drop casting; then dried at 50 ℃ in the oven while the surface and cross-section of composite membrane samples were prepared on copper tape. The prepared samples were coated with gold to ensure the conductive surface and SEM analysis was conducted at voltages of 10 kV for GQDs and 5 kV for composite membranes. The size and morphology of the synthesized GQDs were also obtained using Transmission Electron Microscopy (Titan TEM 300 KV). The surface topography of the pristine and GQDs embedded membranes was analyzed with atomic force microscopy (AFM; Asylum Research MFP30™ Standalone AFM with ARC 2™ Controller, Oxford Instruments Asylum Research, Inc., US). The surface roughness was determined using tip scanning mode. Roughness parameters were obtained based on the built-in mathematical algorithm in the Gwyddion 2.55 software (freeware version; Czech Metrology Institute, Czech Republic).

The aqueous zeta potential of the membranes was determined at room temperature using a zeta potential analyzer (Antonn Par Zeta Potential). The measurements were repeated two times to minimize experimental errors. The hydrophilicity of the fabricated membranes was assessed by the sessile drop goniometer method, based on room temperature static contact angle measurements with a Kruss Easy Drop Analyzer (Kruss, Germany) and deionized water as the wetting liquid. The porosity of the GQDs-embedded PET membranes was calculated using the gravimetric method. The 1 cm2 samples were cut from each membrane and kept for drying at 50 °C for 12 h. After that, samples were wetted entirely with distilled water and shaken for 48 h. The porosity is then calculated using the following equation:

The performance of the fabricated membranes was tested in terms of pure water flux and removal of phenol. The water flux and phenol removal performance were evaluated using a continuous operating semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF setup, shown in the Supplementary data (Fig. S2).

The water flux of pristine and composite membranes was analyzed using a semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF setup with an active membrane area of 28 cm2, and the DI water was pumped through the membrane at a pressure of 1.93 bar (28 psi). The pure water flux was measured using the following equation.

The performance of the pristine and GQDs embedded membranes for aqueous phenol removal was analyzed using the semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF setup (Fig. S2). The feed solution (4 L of 20 ppm phenol solution, pH 9.5) was pumped through the membrane cell with an active area of 28 cm2 at 1.93 bar (28 psi). The phenol concentration in permeate was measured in terms of absorbance using time-dependent off-line UV–vis spectrophotometry, DR6000 benchtop spectrophotometer (HACH, USA). The effect of pH was studied at room temperature by adjusting the feed pH with (0.1 M) HCl and (0.1 M) NaOH. The effectiveness of each membrane in removing phenol was assessed by measuring the phenol concentration in feed and permeate from the calibration curve at specific characteristic absorption wavelengths: 269 nm for pH 2 and 6 and 288 nm for pH 9. The phenol removal performance was then calculated using the following equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the GQDs synthesized from waste carton paper
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR spectroscopy was employed to identify the functional groups present in the GQDs, with the corresponding spectrum shown in Fig. 2-a. The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized GQDs reveals the presence of key functional groups. A broad peak near 3350 cm⁻¹ indicates hydroxyl (-OH) groups, contributing to the hydrophilic nature of the GQDs. Peaks around 2900–3000 cm⁻¹ correspond to C-H stretching from sp² and sp³ hybridized carbon bonds. The sharp peak at ∼ 1660 cm⁻¹ is attributed to carbonyl (CO) groups, likely from carboxyl functionalities at the GQDs' edges. Additionally, the peak near ∼ 1580 cm⁻¹ confirms CC stretching, indicating aromatic domains essential for the graphitic structure which is absent in FTIR spectra of waste carton paper given in Supplementary data (Fig. S3). The presence of a C-O stretching peak at ∼ 1200 cm⁻¹ suggests epoxy or other oxygen-containing groups. These functional groups collectively highlight the oxygen-rich and graphitic nature of the GQDs. Notably, the FTIR spectrum of GQDs synthesized from waste carton paper aligns with previously reported spectra for GQDs derived from citric acid, validating the successful synthesis of GQDs (S. A. and P. B. C., 2020). The summary of the FTIR analysis of GQDs are provided in Table 1. Hence, the FTIR analysis confirms that the synthesized GQDs are rich in OH and COOH groups on their surface. The crystallinity of the GQDs was measured using XRD. The XRD spectrum of synthesized GQDs showed the characteristic peak at 21.5° corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane (Hatefi et al., 2020) (Fig. 2-b). The peak profile for the GQDs XRD patterns has been compatible with the JCPDS card no, 75-0444 (Tajik et al., 2020). The synthesized GQDs exhibit an interlayer spacing of approximately 0.4128 nm, that is more comprehensive than graphite due to the presence of -OH and -COOH functional groups at the edges of GQDs (Pan et al., 2010; Peng et al., 2012). This finding aligns with the reported interlayer spacing values of GQDs synthesized through various methods (Hatefi et al., 2020).

The formation of GQDs from waste carton paper was further confirmed using Raman analysis. The Raman spectrum of the GQDs exhibits the D and G bands at 1349 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1, respectively (Fig. 2-c). D band is a disorder-induced band that confirms the presence of disorder on the edges in the form of sp3 carbons, surface states, or functional groups on the surface of GQDs. G band arises due to the stretching of CC bonds in the sp2 carbon network (S. A. and P. B. C., 2020). The ratio of the intensities of the D and G bands (ID/IG) indicates the level of crystallinity and the amount of core and surface carbon atoms. The synthesized showed an ID/IG value of 0.87. Additionally, the presence of the 2D band at 2680 cm−1 confirms the graphene structure (López et al., 2018).

The TGA analysis was further employed to check the thermal behavior of GQDs over a temperature range between 20 and 1000 °C. The thermogram for the GQDs exhibits three decomposition temperatures (Fig. 2-d). The first weight loss below around 110 °C corresponds to the loss of adsorbed water molecules. The second weight reduction around 220 °C is attributed to eliminating oxygen-containing surface functional groups. The final decomposition above 350 °C is ascribed to the disintegration of the carbon backbone. This decomposition of GQDs with increasing temperature is consistent with the literature (Wang et al., 2017).

Furthermore, the UV-Vis absorption spectra of GQDs and waste cartoon paper are shown in Fig. 2-e and Fig. S4. As seen in Fig. 2-e, GQDs exhibit characteristic absorption peaks corresponding to electronic transitions, primarily involving transitions between π and π* electronic states. The peak at 211 nm is mainly due to π-π* electron transition in CC, whereas the peak at 270 nm corresponds to the electron transition in CO, formed during hydrothermal (Gozali Balkanloo et al., 2023). The emission studies of the GQDs were also done by exciting the UV light on GQDs. For that purpose, UV light (382 nm) was excited on the synthesized GQDs and green fluorescence was observed. However, under natural light, GQDs showed a light-yellow color (Fig. 2-f), consistent with the literature (Abbas et al., 2020).

SEM was employed to determine the morphology and size of the GQDs. Fig. 2-g shows the SEM image of the aggregated GQDs in bigger sizes with a spherical morphology (Hatefi et al., 2020). Since GQDs are highly agglomerated in SEM images, TEM was employed to confirm the size of the single GQDs nanoparticle. As shown in Fig. 2-h, GQDs exist as layers of spherical nanosheets with size in the range of ∼ 46 to ∼ 59 nm with an average calculated size of ∼ 50.5 nm (Supplementary data, Fig. S5). In conclusion, the characterization analysis of synthesized GQDs confirmed that waste carton paper is a potential bio source for GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of pristine and GQDs embedded PET membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-sectional microscopic structures of both pristine and modified PET membranes were examined using SEM to understand the impact of nanofiller addition on the membrane's surface and pore structure. As in Fig. 3-a, the pristine PET membrane has a uniform surface, whereas, in 0.5 wt% GQDs embedded PET membrane has nanofillers on the surface (Fig. 3-b). The GQDs are distributed randomly on the membrane surface. However, with an increased loading of GQDs (0.75 wt% and 1 wt%), the surface became smoother as more GQDs were incorporated within the polymer structure rather than on the membrane surface (Fig. 3. c and d).

The cross-sectional image of the pristine PET membrane (Fig. 3-e) showed a dense structure consisting of globular clusters/beads, and the pores were not interconnected. However, with the addition of GQDs, the structure of polymeric beads was changed to a porous thread-like structure (Fig. 3f–h). This is attributed to the fact that hydrophilic GQDs in the PET polymer matrix caused delayed demixing of solvent and non-solvent during the phase inversion, resulting in a porous structure in the GQDs-PET membranes compared to the dense beads-like structure of pristine membranes. The structural modifications, such as more regular and sponge-like pore structures of PET membranes by adding various additives, are reported in the literature (Kusumocahyo et al., 2020; Tamam Ibnu Ali et al., 2023). Hence, changes in cross-section images and surface images of GQDs-PET membranes compared to pristine PET membranes indicate that hydrophilic GQDs effectively act as pore-forming agents and enhance membrane porosity by creating interconnected pore networks. This kind of change in the structure of PET membranes by GQDs will help to improve the permeability and removal performance of GQDs-PET membranes.

Fig. 4(a–d) illustrates the 5 µm × 5 µm top view AFM maps and corresponding three-dimensional scans of the surface of pristine and GQDs-PET membranes. The topographic contrast can be attributed to texture caused by pores and nanofillers on the surface. The 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane showed higher average surface roughness (91.8 ± 0.48 nm) than other membranes (Fig. 4-e). This higher surface roughness is due to the presence of GQDs on the surface, as shown in surface SEM images (Fig. 3-b). However, as the loading of GQDs increased, the surface roughness of the GQDs-PET membranes decreased, 0.75 and 1 wt% GQDs-PET membranes displayed less average surface roughness, 16.2 ± 1.44 nm and 14.6 ± 0.23 nm, respectively. This decrease in surface roughness can be attributed to fewer GQDs on the surface of the membrane and more inside the polymer matrix of the membrane, as shown in SEM images (Fig. 3e–f). Furthermore, the presence of dark spots in AFM images indicates the pores or voids in the membrane (Fig. 4, a–d). Moreover, the effect of GQDs on PET membranes was also observed in the water contact angle measurements shown in Fig. 4-f. The measurements were taken from three different points on the membrane. The pristine PET membrane showed a water contact angle of 73.4 ± 2.34, attributed to PET's slight hydrophobic nature arising from its molecular structure's non-polar characteristics. However, the addition of GQDs being hydrophilic due to the presence of hydrophilic functional groups (-OH and -COOH) caused a significant decrease in the contact angle of composite membranes. The lowest contact angle (59.1 ± 2.16) was observed in 1 wt% GQDs-PET membrane with the highest concentration of GQDs. Moreover, 0.5 wt% and 0.75 wt% GQDs-PET membranes also showed lower water contact angles than pristine PET membranes, 69.2 ± 1.68 and 67 ± 1.41, respectively (Fig. 4-f).

Furthermore, the loading of GQDs influenced the porosity of the PET membranes. The porosity of the GQDs-PET membranes was calculated using the equation in Section 2.4. The pristine membrane has the least porosity, 22 %, compared to GQDs-embedded PET membranes. As GQDs are introduced in the polymer network of the PET membrane, the porosity of the membrane increases. The porosity of 0.5 wt% and 0.75 and 1 wt% GQDs embedded PET membranes were 26 %, 28 %, and 37 %, respectively (Fig. 4-f). This change in membrane structure was also confirmed by the cross-section SEM images of the GQDs embedded in PET membranes. As the loading of GQDs increases, more GQDs are present in the polymer structure due to the delayed mixing of solvent and non-solvent, resulting in macropores in the membrane structure.

The loading of GQDs also affected the surface charge of the PET membranes. The zeta potential analyses were performed for the pristine PET and composite GQDs-PET membranes to assess their overall surface charge in an aqueous media. The pristine PET membrane and those GQDs displayed negative zeta potential values across different pH values from 3 to 9, indicating a negatively charged surface. (Fig. 4-g). However, the extent of the negative zeta potential in GQDs-PET membranes significantly increased greatly at higher pH (pH ∼ 9) compared to pristine membranes. This is due to the presence of functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl on GQDs, contributing to a more negative surface charge on the membrane surface at higher pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water flux and phenol removal efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: Water permeation is a key performance indicator in developing membranes. Thus, the water permeability of both the pristine and GQDs embedded PET membranes was assessed by calculating the pure water permeance using a semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF system. The membranes were cut for the setup cell with an active area of 28 cm2. The pristine membrane gave low water flux (9.9 L/m2.hr.bar) compared to GQDs-PET membranes due to the dense structure with no pores in the cross section (Fig. 3-e). Also, the percentage porosity of pristine membranes was lower than that of modified PET membranes. However, adding GQDs significantly enhanced the water permeation through the membrane by eliminating the dense polymer structure, introducing pores in the membranes, and lowering the water contact angle (Fig. 4-f). The 0.5 wt% and 0.75 wt% GQDs-PET membrane showed 2.4 and 3.5 times the flux displayed by the pristine membrane, respectively ( Fig. 5-a). Moreover, 1 % GQDs-PET membrane provided 8.4 times the flux of the pristine PET membrane. This significant increase in flux is due to the presence of a high amount of hydrophilic GQDs and a more porous structure as more GQDs are present within the polymer network at 1 wt% GQDs, resulting in macropores, as confirmed in Fig. 3(e–h).

The efficacy of removing small pollutants like phenol is another crucial factor in assessing the effectiveness of advanced membranes. This study processed a model phenol solution with a concentration of 20 ppm (at pH 9.5) using continuous semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF setups, evaluating both pristine PET and composite GQDs-PET membranes with feed solution of 4 L for 90 min (Fig. 5-b). The pristine membrane exhibited high (95.8 ± 0.98 %) removal performance initially; however, the removal performance reduced to only 51.3 ± 0.84 % after 30 min of continuous operation (Fig. 5-b). Moreover, the phenol removal performance decreased further, and only 34 ± 1.54 % phenol removal was observed after 90 minutes. This performance of the pristine membrane can be attributed to the lower negative surface charge at higher pH, as shown in zeta potential measurements (Fig. 4-g), leading to lower rejection and unstable performance in a shorter time. Meanwhile, GQDs embedded PET membranes showed excellent removal performance for a longer duration. The 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane displayed excellent phenol removal (94 ± 1.2 %) throughout the 90 minutes of continuous operation; likewise, the 0.75 wt% GQDs-PET membrane gave slightly less (88 ± 0.96 %) but consistent removal (Fig. 5-b). However, 1 % of the GQDs membrane shows a decrease in removal performance over time. This loss in performance for 1 wt% GQDs-PET membrane could be ascribed to the formation of a highly porous structure (Fig. 3-h & Fig. 4-f) compared to 0.5 wt% and 0.75 wt% GQDs in PET membrane (Bi et al., 2019; Kusumocahyo et al., 2020).

Furthermore, to examine the performance of the membrane at lower pH levels, the experiment was conducted at pH 3 and 6 using the optimal 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane. Fig. 5-d shows that 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane observed decreased removal performance at pH 3 and 6 compared to pH 9.5. At natural or lower pH, phenol is present in its neutral acid form, whereas the GQDs-PET membrane has a lower negative surface charge at this pH compared to higher pH (9.5), indicating that functional groups in the GQDs in the membrane surface exist as more protonated form at lower pH (Fig. 4-g). Hence, the membrane displayed adsorptive interaction, including π-π interaction between π-conjugated structures of GQDs and phenol, and hydrogen bonding interaction of protonated functional groups (-OH and -COOH) in GQDs with phenol.

The leaching of nanofillers from the membrane is essential because it can affect the overall performance of the process. Moreover, the leaching of nanofillers in concentrate or permeate streams will pollute the environment again. Hence, the leaching test of GQDs was performed by keeping a 4 cm2 piece of 0.5 wt% GQDs-PET membrane in 100 ml of water and constant stirring for 48 h. The absorbance of water samples was taken after 48 h using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer (DR-6000, HACH). As shown in Fig. S6, no peak was observed in the UV spectrophotometer for the water sample even after 48 h, confirming that GQDs are perfectly embedded in the PET polymer matrix without any leaching and are highly recommended membranes for membrane-based water treatment.

Table 2 shows a comparison of this work with several other literature studies The phenol rejection rate and water flux of 0.5 wt% GQDs PET membranes exceeded the performance of several nanofillers in polymeric membrane systems. Furthermore, the polymer used in our studies is a recycled PET derived from waste plastic bottles, making the process cost-effective. Moreover, the nanofiller (GQDs) used in this study were also synthesized using waste carton paper, ensuring a cost-effective and sustainable approach. Besides, the long stability and high removal performance displayed in semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF setup for a 4 L volume of feed water open the opportunities for the industrial application of these membranes for the water treatment process. Overall, the GQDs-PET membranes enhanced performance in terms of phenol removal and pure water permeance and longer time stable removal performance.

This study investigated the potential of using waste materials in high-value applications, thereby contributing to the curricular economy by developing sustainable and efficient wastewater treatment technologies. GQDs were successfully synthesized from waste carton paper and incorporated as nanofillers in plastic-derived PET membranes. A comprehensive set of spectroscopy and electron microscopy characterization techniques for GQDs confirmed that waste carton paper is a potential bio source for synthesizing GQDs. The membrane-based characterization data revealed that the weight percent of GQDs influenced the PET membranes' structure, porosity, wetting, permeation properties, and phenol removal efficiency. The optimal GQDs-PET composite membrane showed high flux, superior phenol removal efficiency (94 %) under alkaline conditions (pH∼9.5), and long-time performance stability compared to the pristine PET membrane. The effect of pH and zeta potential studies showed that high negative surface charge, formed by embedding GQDs in PET membranes surface, is responsible for showing longer time stable superior removal performance. Furthermore, the high and stable removal performance of GQDs-PET membranes monitored in a semi-pilot scale cross-flow UF setup will open up opportunities for the industrial application of these membranes for the efficient membrane-based wastewater treatment process. Additionally, this work encouraged sustainable development and circular economy concepts via the reuse of waste plastic bottles and waste carton paper for the application of the wastewater treatment process.
